coredata.dc:title: Bio-inspired molecularly imprinted polymer–green emitting carbon dot composite for selective and sensitive detection of 3-nitrotyrosine as a biomarker
coredata.dc:creator: Jalili, Roghayeh
Amjadi, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent sensor was fabricated for 3-nitrotyrosine (3-NT) by using bio-inspired molecularly imprinted polymer as a receptor and green emitting carbon dots (CDs) as a signal transducer. The sensor was simply prepared by immersing CDs in a weak alkaline solution of dopamine containing 3-NT as template. After extracting the embedded template molecules, imprinted binding sites are left in the composite (BMIP@CDs) that selectively recognize the target molecules. The fluorescence intensity of BMIP@CDs is significantly quenched by 3-NT, which forms the basis for its sensitive detection. The sensor was applied for determination of 3-NT in linear concentration range of 0.050–1.85μM with a detection limit of 17nM. It exhibited excellent selectivity and a high binding affinity to 3-NT over structurally related compounds and utilized for the detection of 3-NT in spiked human serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted polymer
Carbon dots
Sensor
Dopamine
3-Nitrotyrosine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the architecture of the BMIP@CDs sensor and fluorescence quenching mechanism.
Chemical structure of (a) Nitrotyrosine (b) Tyrosine (c) Tryptophan (d) 3-Nitrophenol.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of: (1) CDs (2) BMIP@CDs and (3) BNIP@CDs. (b) SEM and TEM (inset) image of BMIP@CDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the BMIP@CDs sensor upon addition of different concentrations of 3NT (λex =425nm); (b) corresponding calibration graph (Error bars represent one standard deviation for three measurements).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the BNIP@CDs sensor upon addition of different concentrations of 3NT (λex =425nm); (b) corresponding calibration graph. (Error bars represent one standard deviation for three measurements).

(a) Quenching constant of BMIP@CDs and BNIP@CDs for 3-NT and 3-NP. (b) Effect of TYR and (c) TRP on the binding of 3-NT to BMIP@CDs. (Error bars represent one standard deviation for three measurements).
Results for the determination of 3-NT in spiked serum sample.
Comparison between proposed method and some other published methods for the 3-NT determination.

head.ce:title: Bio-inspired molecularly imprinted polymer–green emitting carbon dot composite for selective and sensitive detection of 3-nitrotyrosine as a biomarker

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent sensor was fabricated for 3-nitrotyrosine (3-NT) by using bio-inspired molecularly imprinted polymer as a receptor and green emitting carbon dots (CDs) as a signal transducer. The sensor was simply prepared by immersing CDs in a weak alkaline solution of dopamine containing 3-NT as template. After extracting the embedded template molecules, imprinted binding sites are left in the composite (BMIP@CDs) that selectively recognize the target molecules. The fluorescence intensity of BMIP@CDs is significantly quenched by 3-NT, which forms the basis for its sensitive detection. The sensor was applied for determination of 3-NT in linear concentration range of 0.050–1.85μM with a detection limit of 17nM. It exhibited excellent selectivity and a high binding affinity to 3-NT over structurally related compounds and utilized for the detection of 3-NT in spiked human serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive nitrogen species derived from inflammatory cells can mediate the nitration of tyrosine to form 3-nitrotyrosine (3-NT) [1,2]. A significant increase in the extent of this process results in an increase in 3-NT level in biological samples. Increased level of 3-NT has been associated with a wide range of diseases such as atherosclerosis, osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, Alzheimer, cardiovascular diseases, diseases associated with immunological reactions, diabetes, neurological diseases and psychiatric disorders [3–9]. Thus, 3-NT appears to be a clinically relevant biomarker of nitrosative stress and a sensitive, specific, and reliable method for its monitoring in biological samples is necessary. Three major methods including mass spectrometry (MS) [8], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [10] have been regularly employed to quantify 3-NT both in biological fluids and tissues. Recent reviews summarize the current approaches for determination of this compound in different matrices [11–13].

The main disadvantage of ELISA (the most common and commercialized method) is related to using monoclonal and/or polyclonal natural antibodies against 3-NT. Antibodies are generally limited to physiological conditions and are affected by changes in pH, temperature, or medium thereby reducing lifetime and reproducibility of biosensors. Furthermore, they are expensive, require careful storage and handling and their binding ability can be readily lost [14]. In this regard, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP), the polymeric matrices containing specific recognition sites towards the target molecule (template), have already been introduced as stable artificial biomimetic recognition elements [15,16]. MIPs compared to antibodies have higher physical robustness, strength, resistance to elevated temperature and pressure and inertness towards acids, bases, metal ions and organic solvents. In MIP-based sensors, MIPs are integrated with a transducer component that converts the chemical signal generated upon analyte binding, into an easily quantifiable output signal [17].

Bio-inspired polydopamine (PDA) with excellent biocompatibility, hydrophobicity, biodegradability and the unique adhesive properties is produced by dopamine (DA) self-polymerization and can form films on a wide range of substrates [18,19]. Due to these fantastic features, it has been widely used for various applications including surface modification, metal deposition, drug delivery and others [20–22]. Moreover, since PDA functional groups (amino and catechol groups) and cross-linked structure can generate stable three-dimensional imprinting sites, it is appropriate for molecular imprinting applications [23,24].

Photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs), which are zero dimensional carbon nanomaterials with sizes less than 10nm, have recently attracted considerable attention because of their unique optical and electrochemical properties. Compared with conventional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, photoluminescent CDs are superior in terms of chemical inertness, high photostability, lack of heavy metals, cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility. These features make CDs a very useful tool for biosensing [25]. However, emission wavelength of most reported CDs is located in the blue-light region which hinders their application in the bioimaging and biosensing fields due to the blue auto-fluorescence of biological matrices [26,27].

Currently the main procedure for preparation of MIP-modified quantum dots is sol-gel process using 3-(aminopropyl)triethoxysilane or trimethoxyphenylsilane, as functional monomers and tetraethyl orthosilicate as a cross-linker [28–30]. Difficulty in controlling the particle size, morphology, and monodispersity and also limited number of silica precursors available are the main disadvantages of the sol- gel process [31]. Herein, for the first time, we used PDA-based molecularly imprinted polymer for coating CDs. PDA-based molecular recognition sites were formed on green emitting CDs for recognition of 3-NT. This is the first quantum dot-based sensor reporting for 3-NT. The prepared bio-inspired molecularly imprinted polymer-CD composite (BMIP@CDs) showed a good sensitivity and selectivity and rapid response toward 3-NT and was applied for florescent sensing of 3-NT in biological samples. The proposed preparation procedure for composite is a simple and versatile imprinting method, which is applicable to a wide variety of analytes.

All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used as received. Tyrosine (TYR), tryptophan (TRP) and 3-nitrophenole (3-NP) were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). 3-Nitrotyrosine (3-NT) and dopamine hydrochloride (DA) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (US). 3-NT stock solution with concentration of 0.5mM was prepared in 0.1M NaOH solution and stored at 4°C. Working solutions were prepared daily by further dilution of the stock solution with water. Ultra-pure water obtained from aqua MAX − Ultra 370 water purification system (Young Lin instrument Co., Korea) was used for all experiments.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu RF-540 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan) equipped with a quartz cell (1cm×1cm). The slit widths of excitation and emission monochromators were both set at 10nm. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by a Cary-100 spectrophotometer (Varian, Australia). All optical measurements were carried out under ambient temperature. The size and structure of BMIP@CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (BioTwin and CM 120, Philips, Netherlands) and scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Mira 3 FEG, Tescan Co., Czech Republic). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) measurement was carried out with a Tensor-27 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker Co., Germany).

Green emitting CDs were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method as described previously in the literature [32]. Briefly, 0.7g glucose was dissolved in 2.0mL of water and mixed with 5.0mL of concentrated phosphoric acid. The mixture was then placed in an oven and heated at 90°C for 30min to obtain CDs. After cooling, the sample was centrifuged at 6000rpm for 30min to remove large particles. Then the pH was neutralized with NaOH (1M).

In a typical synthesis, 1.0mL of as-prepared CDs was added to a mixture of 15mL Tris buffer (10mM, pH=8.5) and 5.0mL isopropyl alcohol with stirring. After adding 3-NT (20mg) and DA (7mg), the reaction was continued for 12h at room temperature [33,34]. The resultant imprinted nanoparticles, were washed with water to remove the unreacted monomer. Subsequently, they were washed with methanol several times to extract the template. The removal of 3-NT was checked by measuring UV absorption of supernatant solution. The obtained BMIP@CDs were re-dispersed in water (50mL) and stored at 4°C. For comparison, non-imprinted nanoparticles (BNIP@CDs) were also prepared in the same conditions but without the template.

To a 5.0mL volumetric flask, 300μL of stock solution of BMIP@CDs, 500μL of phosphate buffer solution (pH=6.0, 0.1M), and 3.5mL of 3-NT sample or standard solution were added and the volume was reached to 5.0mL with deionized water. After incubation for 8min, the fluorescence intensity was measured at 520nm with an excitation wavelength of 425nm in a 1-cm standard cuvette.

Human serum samples were obtained from Blood Transfusion Center (Tabriz, Iran). A 1.0-mL aliquot of each sample was transferred to a centrifuge tube and spiked by adding appropriate volumes of 3-NT standard solution. Then, 0.5mL acetonitrile was added into the tube in order to precipitate proteins. After centrifugation for 20min, the clear supernatant was diluted to 5.0mL in a volumetric flask and analyzed according to the general procedure.

Our fabrication procedure for the bio-inspired imprinted polymer-CD composite (BMIP@CDs) is illustrated in Scheme 1 . It has previously been demonstrated that a simple immersion of semiconductor or graphene quantum dots in a DA solution leads to spontaneous deposition of PDA layer on them [33,35]. We used a similar procedure in the presence of template (3-NT) to obtain MIP layer on CDs. During the self-polymerization of dopamine on the CDs, 3-NT molecules are embedded in the cross-linked polymeric network via the non-covalent interaction of the amine and hydroxyl groups on PDA chain. The imprinted BMIP@CDs material is obtained after the removal of the embedded template. During sensing step, 3-NT is captured by the recognition sites of the BMIP@CDs and causes a decrease in the fluorescence intensity due to strong quenching characteristic of the molecule.

One of the interesting and well-known properties of PDA is its strong adhesive ability to various surfaces regardless of the substrate’s chemistry [18]. Depending on the surface properties, the adhesion of PDA to the substrate can be either through covalent binding or noncovalent binding. Covalent binding may occur via Michael addition or Schiff base reaction in substrates containing amine or thiol groups on their surfaces. On the other hand, PDA can stick to various substrates through noncovalent binding interactions such as metal coordination, hydrogen bonding, π−π stacking and charge-transfer complex formation [18]. In our sensing system, it seems that MIP layer and CDs are held together by hydrogen bonding and/or π−π stacking. In order to show the successful coating of PDA on CDs, FTIR spectra were recorded. As shown in Fig. 2a, the green emitting CDs, show a broad peak at 3445cm−1 which is the characteristic peak of hydroxyl groups. The band at 1650cm−1 could be assigned to the vibration of carboxyl or carbonyl groups. On the other hand, in the FT-IR spectra of BMIP@CDs and BNIP@CDs, absorption bands related to catechol and amine groups are present which confirms the successful coating of PDA on CDs. The absorption peak at ca. 1465cm−1 is ascribed to NH scissoring. Sharp absorption bands at about 3493cm−1 (stretching vibration of phenolic OH and NH) and 1645cm−1 (stretching vibration of aromatic ring and bending vibration of NH), all prove the existence of PDA. BMIP@CDs and BNIP@CDs exhibit major bands in similar locations, because the compositions of them are similar.

The morphology of the BMIP@CDs was studied by SEM. As shown in Fig. 2b, these nanoparticles are monodisperse and spherical with an approximate diameter of 130nm and rough surfaces. The TEM image showed similar results (Fig. 2b inset).

When excited in the wavelength range of 380–500nm, the prepared BMIP@CDs exhibit an excitation-independent photoluminescence centered at 520nm. Meanwhile, the strongest emission peak corresponds to the excitation wavelength of 425nm. Unlike most previously reported CD-based probes that have blue emission and ultraviolet excitation, the higher excitation and emission wavelengths of this probe, reduces the interference by auto-fluorescence of biological matrix.
In order to obtain a high sensitivity for 3-NT quantification, assay conditions which may affect the response of BMIP@CDs, such as the pH, interaction time and amount of probe were optimized.

The influence of interaction time between the analyte and BMIP@CDs on the fluorescence intensity is shown in Fig. S1 (Electronic Supplementary Materials, ESM). The relative intensity (F0/F) increased up to 8.0min, then the reaction reached equilibrium and the curve became flat. Therefore, 8.0min was selected as the optimum interaction time for the following experiments. Compared to most conventional silica-based imprinted QDs, which normally need 20–60min to reach the equilibrium [36–38], this sensor has an extraordinary rapid response, which is probably due to the high porosity of PDA as seen in SEM image (Fig. 2b).

The pH of solution plays an important role in the sensing process because it affects the fluorescence intensity of BMIP@CDs as well as the interactions between 3-NT and BMIP@CDs. The fluorescence intensity of BMIP@CDs is considerably stable in the range of 6.0–11.0 but decreases at lower pH values. So the effect of pH on the fluorescence response of the probe in the presence of 3-NT was studied in the range of 6.0–11.0. The pH was adjusted by using dilute NaOH and HCl solutions. The results are shown in Table S1 (ESM). The maximum fluorescence quenching occurs at pH 6.0–7.0. This can be explained by considering the pK a values of 3-NT (2.2, 7.2 and 9.11 [39]) and pH pzc of PDA (4.0 [24]). PDA is negatively charged at pH values higher than 4 and 3-NT molecules have also a net negative charge above pH 7. Therefore by increasing the pH of solution, repulsion between PAD layer and 3-NT would prevent 3-NT from penetration to binding sites, leading to a decrease in quenching. We used a phosphate buffer solution (0.01M, pH 6.0) for further experiments to obtain a stable fluorescence intensity and high sensitivity.

The effect of amount of BMIP@CDs was studied by changing their volume from 50 to 400μL. As shown in Fig. S2 (ESM) the highest quenching effect appeared at 300μL. Therefore, the amount of BMIP@CDs was fixed at 300μL throughout the work.

The ability of the BMIP@CD sensor for quantification of 3-NT was evaluated in the optimum conditions. The fluorescence intensity of the BMIP@CDs decreases significantly by increasing 3-NT concentration (Fig. 3a). The fluorescence quenching follows the Stern-Volmer equation:
F0/F=1+KSV [3-NT]
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the BMIP@CDs in the absence and presence of 3-NT, respectively, KSV is the Stern–Volmer constant, and [3-NT] is the quencher concentration.

The plot of F0/F as function of [3-NT] is shown in Fig. 3b. Ksv value is found to be 909855M−1. The linear range of the calibration curve is 0.050–1.85μM (R2 =0.9978). The limit of detection evaluated by 3σ criteria, is 17nM (3.8μgL−1), which is lower than the level of 3-NT in many disease [5–7]. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for 5 replicate determinations of 3-NT (0.45μM) was 3.6%, which indicates the good repeatability of the sensor. The fluorescence stability of BMIP@CDs was studied by the measurement of its fluorescence intensity during several days. It was found that when BMIP@CD solution is stored at 4°C it is stable for more than two weeks The fluorescence intensity was 95.1% of its initial value after three weeks.

The imprinting effect of the BMIP@CDs was confirmed by a control experiment with BNIP@CDs under the same experimental conditions (Fig. 4a). As can be seen, KSV,NIP is substantially smaller than KSV,MIP indicating the lower binding affinity of 3-NT to BNIP@CDs. As shown in Fig. 4b, the sensitivity is much lower and the linear range is narrower (0.075–1.5μM with a detection limit of 49nM) than BMIP@CD sensor. The imprinting factor (IF, the ratio of KSV,MIP to KSV,NIP) was calculated to be 2.78.

After adding 3-NT to BMIP@CDs, the multifunctional binding sites on the imprinted BMIP@CDs could recognize the template via interactions such as hydrogen bond formation and van der Waals forces. As a result of the strong electron-withdrawing power of the NO2 group quenching of CDs fluorescence by 3-NT is anticipated [40]. We suggest that photoinduced electron transfer from excited-state CDs to 3-NT is responsible for the fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence in the CDs is believed to arise from radiative recombination of the electron-hole pairs. When there is no 3-NT around the BMIP@CDs, a green emission is generated at 520nm. After adding 3-NT, the amine and hydroxyl groups in the binding sites of MIP layer, can interact with the template molecule to form a complex through hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces. Such strong interaction between 3-NT and BMIP@CDs facilitates the nonradiative deactivation of excited-state CDs via electron transfer process. As shown in Fig. S3 (ESM), the UV–vis absorption band of 3-NT is close to the band gap of the CDs. Therefore, as illustrated in Scheme 1, electrons in the conduction band of the excited CDs can transfer to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of 3-NT molecules as electron acceptors, leading to the quenching of CDs fluorescence. On the other hand, energy transfer is not a possible mechanism for fluorescence quenching because there is no spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of 3-NT and emission spectrum of CDs.

In the next step, the interference effect of two amino acid TYR and TRP (Fig. 1), which have similar structure with 3-NT and always coexist with it in biological samples was studied. The competitive binding was performed by preparing a series of solutions with a constant concentration of 3-NT and increasing concentrations of TYR or TRP. As shown in Fig. 5b and c the fluorescence quenching is not affected by increasing the CTYR/C3NT or CTRP/C3NT ratios. This indicates that the recognition of BMIP@CDs is based on the functionality, shape and size of the template and is selective to 3-NT.

Finally, the effect of some potentially interfering species was studied. For each interferent, a solution containing 0.45μM of 3-NT and 450μM of interefrent was prepared and its fluorescence response was measured. It was found that the following species do not cause more than 5% relative error in the fluorescence response at molar ratios of 1000-fold: sucrose, glucose, lactose, uric acid, ascorbic acid, l-cysteine, glycine, creatinine, Na+, K+, NH4 +, Ag+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cl−, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, F−, NO3 −.

To evaluate the practical applicability and accuracy of the presented sensor, it was applied for the determination of 3-NT in human serum samples. Recovery studies were carried out by spiking the samples with 3-NT in the concentration range of 0.4–1.0μM. A summary of the calculated mean concentration for each sample is shown in Table 1 . The obtained recoveries are between 95.6% and 101.2%, with RSDs in the range of 1.4% to 3.9%. These results confirm that BMIP@CDs can be applied for detection of 3-NT in real samples.

We here reported for the first time a selective CD-based fluorescent sensor for 3-NT by using bio-inspired MIP-CD composite. The sensor shows a bright green emission which is selectively quenched by 3-NT probably via photoinduced electron transfer process. Comparison of the proposed method with some other reported analytical methods for the 3-NT quantification is shown in Table 2 . Our probe has comparable sensitivity with some ELISA methods and better sensitivity than some reported HPLC methods. Although mass spectrometric methods have much higher sensitivity but they involve expensive instruments and time-consuming procedures. A fluorescent sensor has also been reported for 3-NT [7], which has higher sensitivity than our method but involves a tedious and time-consuming procedure for synthesis of a binuclear Pt complex as fluorescent reagent. Our developed method is very fast and simple with respect other methods. The senor has a good selectivity and can be applied to detection of μM levels of 3-NT in serum samples.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.08.145.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous detection of 3-nitrotyrosine and 3-nitro-4-hydroxyphenylacetic acid in human urine by online SPE LC-MS/MS and their association with oxidative and methylated DNA lesions
Tyrosine nitration as mediator of cell death
3-Nitrotyrosine: A biomarker of nitrogen free radical species modified proteins in systemic autoimmunogenic conditions
Cerebrospinal fluid levels of free 3-nitrotyrosine are not elevated in the majority of patients with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis or Alzheimerös disease
Detection of nitrotyrosine in the diabetic plasma: evidence of oxidative stress
Effects of rosuvastatin on 3-nitrotyrosine and aortic stiffness in hypercholesterolemia

Nano optical sensor binuclear Pt-2-pyrazinecarboxylic acid −bipyridine for enhancement of the efficiency of 3-nitrotyrosine biomarker for early diagnosis of liver cirrhosis with minimal hepatic encephalopathy
A novel mixed-mode solid phase extraction coupled with LC-MS/MS for the re-evaluation of free 3-nitrotyrosine in human plasma as an oxidative stress biomarker
Development of a new HPLC-based method for 3-nitrotyrosine quantification in different biological matrices
Establishment of a sandwich ELISA using commercial antibody for plasma or serum 3-nitrotyrosine (3NT). Elevation in inflammatory diseases and complementary between 3NT and myeloperoxidase
3-Nitrotyrosine quantification methods: current concepts and future challenges
Recent methodological advances in the mass spectrometric analysis of free and protein-associated 3-nitrotyrosine in human plasma
Mass spectrometry and 3-nitrotyrosine: strategies controversies, and our current perspective
A comparison of the performance of molecularly imprinted polymer nanoparticles for small molecule targets and antibodies in the ELISA format
Bio-mimetic sensors based on molecularly imprinted membranes
A sensitive fluorescent nanosensor for chloramphenicol based on molecularly imprinted polymer-capped CdTe quantum dots
Advancements in nanosensors using plastic antibodies
Polydopamine and its derivative materials: synthesis and promising applications in energy, environmental, and biomedical fields
Selective determination of atropine using poly dopamine-coated molecularly imprinted Mn-Doped ZnS quantum dots
Surface modification of hybrid-fabric composites with amino silane and polydopamine for enhanced mechanical and tribological behaviors
Polydopamine-based simple and versatile surface modification of polymeric nano drug carriers
Metal deposition by electroless plating on polydopamine functionalized micro- and nanoparticles
A resonance light scattering sensor based on bioinspired molecularly imprinted polymers for selective detection of papain at trace levels
Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications
Surface molecular imprinting on silane-functionalized carbon dots for selective recognition of nifedipine
Carbon dots serve as an effective probe for the quantitative determination and for intracellular imaging of mercury(II)
Molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica embedded with carbon dots and semiconductor quantum dots as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for diniconazole
Dummy molecularly imprinted polymers-capped CdTe quantum dots for the fluorescent sensing of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene
Quantum dots based mesoporous structured imprinting microspheres for the sensitive fluorescent detection of phycocyanin
Controlling morphology and porosity to improve performance of molecularly imprinted sol–gel silica
Surface coating of graphene quantum dots using mussel-inspired polydopamine for biomedical optical imaging
Polydopamine spheres as active templates for convenient synthesis of various nanostructures
Attenuation of the in vivo toxicity of biomaterials by polydopamine surface modification
A novel dual-function molecularly imprinted polymer on CdTe/ZnS quantum dots for highly selective and sensitive determination of ractopamine
Fluorescent turn-on detection of cysteine using a molecularly imprinted polyacrylate linked to allylthiol-capped CdTe quantum dots
Development of fluorescence sensing material based on CdSe/ZnS quantum dots and molecularly imprinted polymer for the detection of carbaryl in rice and chinese cabbage
Large-volume sample stacking-capillary electrophoresis used for the determination of 3-nitrotyrosine in rat urine
Simultaneous determination of 3-nitrotyrosine o-, m-, and p-tyrosine in urine samples by liquid chromatography-ultraviolet absorbance detection with pre-column cloud point extraction
Immunoaffinity liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry detection of nitrotyrosine in biological fluids: development of a clinically translatable biomarker
Developments in the synthesis of a water compatible molecularly imprinted polymer as artificial receptor for detection of 3-nitro-l-tyrosine in neurological diseases

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Roghayeh Jalili is currently a PhD student at the University of Tabriz. Her studies focused on the development of new MIP-modified semiconductor and carbon quantum dots for biomedical and environmental sensing.

Mohammad Amjadi is professor of Analytical Chemistry at the University of Tabriz. His current research interest includes the use of carbon nanomaterials, quantum dots and plasmonic nanoparticles to design new optical detection strategies for pharmaceutical, biological and environmental applications. He is also interested in development of fluorescence and chemiluminescence-based analytical methods.
